summary: Epic Dark Star > Shakedown Street jam marathon
blurb: The second set rolls like a wave of fire with a soaring Dark Star and blistering Shakedown Street that melt into an hour‑long jam through Man Smart, Woman Smarter, The Other One and Standing on the Moon. Jerry’s solos are razor‑sharp, Phil’s bass is a living organism, and the band rides a tight groove from start to finish.
review: The March 20, 1992 Copps Coliseum show captures the Grateful Dead in top form during what many fans call a “tour‑de‑force” second set. After a solid warm‑up first set featuring Althea and The Same Thing, the band launches into an unforgettable Shakedown Street that segues straight into Man Smart, Woman Smarter before exploding into a monumental Dark Star. That Dark Star flows seamlessly into a tight Drums/Space stretch and then into one of the night’s high points – an expansive The Other One that rides the groove all the way to Standing on the Moon and a rousing Turn On Your Lovelight. Jerry Garcia delivers “ridiculously good” solos on both Shakedown Street and Dark Star, his vocal phrasing spot‑on despite a few mix quirks in one SBD source. Phil Lesh’s bass is described as a living organism that never thins out, while the dual drummers provide some of the most satisfying kick‑and‑space passages heard on this tour. Keyboard work shines with Vince Welnick’s tasteful playing and Bruce Hornsby’s accordion accents and piano solo adding extra color. The crystal‑clear Gardner soundboard recording is the definitive source, but an excellent matrix version offers a balanced stereo image for those who prefer a fuller crowd feel.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All six analyzed recordings carry a positive sentiment, repeatedly praising Dark Star, Shakedown Street, The Other One and the second‑set jam flow. Multiple sources call the performance "epic," "blistering," and note Jerry’s solos as "ridiculously good" while highlighting Phil’s bass as a "living organism" and drums/space passages as standout moments. No negative comments about musical execution appear, just occasional mix issues, so a strong positive adjustment is warranted.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard capture with excellent instrument separation; showcases the full detail of Jerry’s solos, Phil’s bass and the second‑set jam marathon.

key_highlights: Epic Dark Star that builds into a flawless jam through The Other One.
Blistering Shakedown Street solo work and seamless segue into Man Smart, Woman Smarter.
Strong first‑set warm‑up with Althea, The Same Thing and Bird Song setting the stage.
Crystal‑clear Gardner SBD provides the most detailed listening experience.

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers razor‑sharp solos on Shakedown Street and Dark Star; vocal phrasing is spot‑on despite a few mix hiccups early in the show.
band_performance.Phil: Bass described as a "living organism" that drives the set without ever getting thin, anchoring the long jams.
band_performance.Bob: Adds quirky rhythm shouts and feedback/warble chords, especially effective on U.S. Blues.
band_performance.Mickey: Drums provide powerful kick and dynamic space passages; the dual‑drum interplay is highlighted as one of the tour’s best.
band_performance.Billy: Shares the drum duties with Mickey, contributing to the tight, flowing rhythm section throughout the set.
